events.text: The patient is a 66-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She has a past medical history significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet-controlled), hypertension, and a 15-pack-year smoking history (quit 10 years prior). She has no family history of pancreatic cancer. She is of Ghanaian descent and works as a retired librarian.

At age 66 years, the patient presented to her primary care physician with a 10-week history of progressive painless jaundice, 14-pound unintentional weight loss, new-onset epigastric discomfort radiating to the back, and pale, greasy stools. On review of systems she also reported pruritus and dark urine. Physical examination revealed scleral icterus, mild hepatomegaly, and a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign). Laboratory evaluation showed total bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 6.9 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, AST 98 U/L, ALT 112 U/L, CA 19-9 1,204 U/mL, CEA 7.1 ng/mL, and albumin 3.1 g/dL. She was referred urgently to gastroenterology and hepatobiliary surgery.

At age 66 years, the patient had a CT abdomen and pelvis with triple-phase contrast protocol (pancreatic protocol CT), which showed a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas causing upstream pancreatic ductal dilation to 7 mm and common bile duct dilation to 16 mm. The mass abutted the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over 180 degrees of its circumference and involved the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with focal narrowing. No encasement of the celiac axis or hepatic artery was seen. No discrete hepatic or peritoneal metastases were identified. Two sub-centimeter peripancreatic lymph nodes were noted (largest 0.9 cm). Findings were consistent with borderline resectable pancreatic head mass by AHPBA/SSO/SSAT criteria.

At age 66 years, the patient had an MRI abdomen with MRCP, which confirmed the 3.8 cm pancreatic head mass with SMA abutment (contact over approximately 170 degrees) and SMV involvement with venous narrowing but without complete occlusion. No hepatic lesions. No ascites. The main pancreatic duct measured 6.5 mm in the body. Findings corroborated borderline resectable status. No distant metastatic disease was identified.

At age 66 years, the patient had an endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy of the pancreatic head mass. Pathology revealed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Tumor cells were arranged in infiltrative glandular structures with desmoplastic stroma. Immunohistochemistry showed positivity for CK7, CK19, MUC1, and CA 19-9, and negativity for CK20 and CDX2. The Ki-67 proliferation index was approximately 35%. No lymphovascular invasion was identified in the biopsy specimen. The morphology was consistent with conventional pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, excluding acinar cell carcinoma and neuroendocrine tumor.

At age 66 years, the patient received a diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, clinical stage T3 N1 M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage IIB). Primary site: Pancreatic head (C25.0). Histology: Adenocarcinoma, NOS (8140/3). Summary stage: Regional by direct extension (SMA abutment) and regional lymph nodes involved (peripancreatic adenopathy). Site-specific factors: tumor size 3.8 cm, SMA abutment without encasement, SMV involvement with narrowing, biliary obstruction present, CA 19-9 1,204 U/mL, CEA 7.1 ng/mL. Performance status ECOG 1. Confirmation: Positive histology (EUS-guided biopsy).

At age 66 years, the patient was discussed at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. Given borderline resectable disease with SMA abutment and SMV involvement, consensus recommendation was for neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy with FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin, irinotecan, leucovorin, fluorouracil) followed by reassessment for chemoradiation and surgical resection. ERCP with biliary stent placement was recommended prior to initiation of chemotherapy given significant obstructive jaundice (bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL). Performance status was deemed adequate for doublet or triplet regimen. Patient expressed strong preference to proceed with aggressive curative-intent therapy and consented to the treatment plan.

At age 66 years, the patient underwent ERCP with placement of a fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS) for palliation of malignant biliary obstruction. Procedure was uncomplicated. Post-procedure bilirubin declined to 1.8 mg/dL within two weeks, and liver function tests normalized, allowing safe initiation of chemotherapy.

At age 66 years, the patient initiated neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (modified): oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 (reduced from 180 mg/m2 due to biliary stent and Gilbert syndrome concern), and 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 as a 46-hour continuous infusion, administered every 14 days. Neulasta (pegfilgrastim) prophylaxis given with each cycle. Treatment intent: neoadjuvant (curative). Cycles 1 through 8 planned.

At age 66 years, during neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (cycles 1-3), the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy affecting the fingertips and toes (CTCAE v5 grade 2), grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 nausea adequately controlled with ondansetron and prochlorperazine, and grade 1 alopecia. She also experienced one episode of grade 2 diarrhea during cycle 2 that was managed with loperamide and temporary dose hold. No febrile neutropenia occurred. CA 19-9 after cycle 2 had declined to 580 U/mL. Dose reductions were not required.

At age 66 years, after 4 cycles of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX, the patient had a restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol. The pancreatic head mass had decreased to 3.0 cm (from 3.8 cm, representing a 21% decrease in maximal diameter). The degree of SMA abutment had decreased to approximately 120 degrees. The SMV narrowing had improved. No new hepatic or peritoneal lesions. CA 19-9 at this timepoint was 310 U/mL. Findings were consistent with partial response per RECIST 1.1. Recommendation was to continue FOLFIRINOX for an additional 4 cycles.

At age 66 years, during neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (cycles 5-8), the patient's grade 2 peripheral neuropathy progressed to grade 3 (interfering with activities of daily living), prompting discontinuation of oxaliplatin after cycle 6. She completed cycles 7 and 8 with fluorouracil/leucovorin/irinotecan (FOLFIRI) backbone only. Grade 2 fatigue persisted. She lost an additional 6 pounds during this phase. No hospitalizations were required.

At age 66 years, after completing 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (6 cycles FOLFIRINOX, 2 cycles FOLFIRI), restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol demonstrated the primary pancreatic head mass measuring 2.6 cm (down from 3.8 cm at baseline), with SMA abutment now less than 90 degrees. The SMV appeared patent without significant narrowing. No distant metastatic disease. CA 19-9 was 85 U/mL. Overall response assessment: partial response. The multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed the case and recommended proceeding with chemoradiation prior to surgical resection to further sterilize surgical margins and address potential micrometastatic disease.

At age 66 years, the patient was presented again at the pancreatic tumor board following completion of 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy. CA 19-9 had declined from 1,204 to 85 U/mL (93% decline). Imaging demonstrated partial response with reduction in SMA abutment to less than 90 degrees and SMV patency restored. Peripheral neuropathy had improved to grade 1 over the 4-week break. Performance status ECOG 1. The board recommended consolidation chemoradiation with capecitabine as a radiosensitizer, followed by surgical resection (Whipple procedure/pancreaticoduodenectomy) approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation. Patient agreed to proceed.

At age 66 years, the patient received stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) to the primary pancreatic tumor and regional nodal stations. Total dose: 54 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) using IMRT technique with simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) to the gross tumor volume. Concurrent capecitabine 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days was administered as a radiosensitizer. Radiation fields included the primary tumor with 1 cm margin, celiac axis nodal region, and peripancreatic lymph nodes. Treatment duration: 5.5 weeks. Stereotactic image guidance (CBCT) used daily. The patient tolerated treatment reasonably well with grade 1 nausea, grade 1 radiation dermatitis, and mild fatigue.

At age 66 years, during chemoradiation, the patient experienced grade 2 nausea and anorexia requiring antiemetic intensification with olanzapine 5 mg nightly added to ondansetron. She lost an additional 4 pounds. Grade 1 radiation-induced dermatitis over the epigastric region was managed with topical emollients. Grade 1 diarrhea occurred during weeks 3-4, self-limited with dietary modification. No treatment interruptions were required. Capecitabine hand-foot syndrome was grade 1 only.

At age 66 years, approximately 5 weeks after completing chemoradiation, the patient underwent repeat CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol for pre-surgical assessment. The pancreatic head mass measured 2.3 cm with further reduction in SMA abutment (estimated contact less than 60 degrees). The SMV was patent. No new hepatic lesions or peritoneal implants were identified. CA 19-9 was 52 U/mL. The surgical team deemed the patient a candidate for attempted curative resection with Whipple procedure. PET-CT was additionally obtained showing no FDG-avid distant metastases, with moderate FDG uptake in the primary pancreatic mass (SUVmax 4.1, decreased from prior).

At age 66 years, the patient underwent open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (PPPD, Whipple procedure) with en bloc superior mesenteric vein (SMV) segmental resection and primary venous reconstruction. Intraoperatively, the tumor was found to be densely adherent to the SMV over a 2 cm segment requiring resection with end-to-end anastomosis. The SMA was carefully dissected and preserved with adequate margins. Total operative time was 7 hours and 40 minutes. Estimated blood loss was 820 mL; the patient received 2 units of packed red blood cells intraoperatively. A pylorus-preserving reconstruction was achieved with hepaticojejunostomy, pancreaticojejunostomy (duct-to-mucosa), and duodenojejunostomy. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin were negative. The common bile duct margin and portal vein margin were also negative on frozen section.

At age 66 years, the patient had the surgical resection specimen evaluated by anatomic pathology. Final pathology of the Whipple resection specimen: moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, 2.1 cm in greatest dimension, involving the pancreatic head and uncinate process. Extensive treatment effect (fibrosis, calcifications, necrosis) with College of American Pathologists (CAP) tumor regression grade 2 (moderate response: residual cancer with evident tumor regression, but more than single cells or rare small groups of cancer cells). Lymphovascular invasion: absent. Perineural invasion: present. Margins: pancreatic neck margin negative (clearance 3 mm), common bile duct margin negative (clearance 5 mm), retroperitoneal/uncinate margin (SMA margin) negative (clearance 1.2 mm), SMV margin negative (clearance 2 mm) following vascular resection. Total lymph nodes examined: 22; metastatic carcinoma in 1 of 22 lymph nodes (1/22). Pathologic stage: ypT3 N1 M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage IIB). R0 resection confirmed.

At age 66 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing (NGS) performed on the resected Whipple specimen using a comprehensive genomic profiling panel (tumor tissue, 648-gene panel). Specimen: primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (pancreatic head, surgical resection). Tumor mutational burden: 3 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Copy number: no high-level amplifications. Loss of heterozygosity (LOH): low. Actionable alterations: KRAS p.G12V mutation (variant allele frequency 42%, pathogenic), TP53 p.R175H mutation (VAF 38%, pathogenic). Additional alterations of potential significance: SMAD4 loss (homozygous deletion, likely driver), CDKN2A/p16 homozygous deletion. Other detected alterations: ARID1A p.Q556* truncating mutation (VAF 18%), KDM6A missense variant of uncertain significance. Fusions: none detected. NTRK1/2/3 fusion: not detected. BRCA1/2: no pathogenic mutations detected (germline testing recommended given family history evaluation - result: germline BRCA1/2 negative confirmed on separate germline assay). ATM: no pathogenic mutation. PALB2: no pathogenic mutation. HER2 amplification: not detected. PD-L1 expression by IHC: 1% (low). Clinical interpretation: KRAS G12V-mutant, TP53-mutant, SMAD4-deleted, CDKN2A-deleted pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; microsatellite stable, low TMB; no currently actionable targets for approved targeted therapies; germline testing negative.

At age 66 years, on postoperative day 5 following the Whipple procedure, the patient developed a pancreatic fistula (postoperative pancreatic fistula, POPF). Drain amylase on postoperative day 3 was 4,280 U/L (greater than 3x upper limit of normal serum amylase), consistent with International Study Group on Pancreatic Fistula (ISGPF) Grade B pancreatic fistula. The patient required prolonged drain maintenance, NPO status with transition to total parenteral nutrition (TPN), and octreotide 200 mcg subcutaneously three times daily. She was ultimately discharged home on postoperative day 14 with Jackson-Pratt drain in situ and outpatient wound care. The drain was removed at 6 weeks post-surgery after confirmation of fistula closure by sinogram.

At age 66 years (approximately 10 weeks post-surgery), the patient was seen in follow-up by medical oncology. She had recovered from the pancreatic fistula with drain removed 2 weeks prior. Performance status ECOG 1. She had regained 4 pounds. She reported persistent grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in the fingertips (attributed to prior oxaliplatin). She had developed new-onset exocrine pancreatic insufficiency requiring pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with pancrelipase 72,000 units with meals. Her pre-existing type 2 diabetes had worsened post-pancreatectomy and now required metformin plus basal insulin glargine. CA 19-9 was 18 U/mL (within normal limits). CEA was 2.4 ng/mL. Discussion of adjuvant chemotherapy was initiated: recommendation for gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap regimen) per the ESPAC-4 trial evidence.

At age 67 years (approximately 12 weeks post-surgery), the patient initiated adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap). Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 intravenously on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle; capecitabine 1660 mg/m2/day orally in two divided doses on days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle. Treatment intent: adjuvant (curative). Planned duration: 6 cycles (24 weeks). Dose reductions applied: capecitabine started at 80% dose (1330 mg/m2/day) given prior chemoradiation, post-surgical debility, and ongoing neuropathy concern.

At age 67 years, during adjuvant GemCap (cycles 1-3), the patient experienced recurrence of the pancreatic fistula (Grade A, minor leak) detected by rising drain output on cycle 1 day 10. Gemcitabine was temporarily held for 2 weeks. The leak resolved spontaneously with conservative management (clear liquids, drain irrigation, octreotide). Capecitabine caused grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) requiring dose reduction to 75% (1247 mg/m2/day) and topical urea cream. Grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 nausea were also present. Gemcitabine-associated cytopenias: grade 2 thrombocytopenia (nadir platelet count 68,000/mcL) and grade 2 anemia (hemoglobin nadir 8.9 g/dL) requiring gemcitabine day 15 dose omission in cycles 2 and 3.

At age 67 years, after completing 3 cycles of adjuvant GemCap, the patient had a restaging evaluation. CA 19-9 remained at 22 U/mL (stable, normal range). CEA 2.1 ng/mL. Performance status ECOG 1-2. The fistula recurrence had resolved. Gemcitabine was dose-reduced to 75% (750 mg/m2) due to cumulative myelosuppression. Capecitabine dose was maintained at 75%. The patient expressed significant fatigue and desire to reduce treatment intensity, but agreed to complete the planned 6 cycles. Discussion of treatment goals and quality of life was documented. Diabetes management was ongoing with endocrinology co-management.

At age 67 years, during adjuvant GemCap cycles 4-6, the patient developed grade 1 peripheral neuropathy worsening (now grade 2, attributed to additive capecitabine neurotoxicity on background oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy). Grade 2 fatigue continued. Grade 1 mucositis occurred during cycle 5. Platelet counts recovered to above 100,000/mcL with dose-reduced gemcitabine. No further episodes of fistula leak. The patient completed all 6 cycles of adjuvant GemCap, with cycles 4-6 administered at reduced doses (gemcitabine 75%, capecitabine 75%). Final CA 19-9 at end of adjuvant treatment: 19 U/mL.

At age 67 years, upon completion of adjuvant GemCap (approximately 8 months post-surgery), the patient had surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast. No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. The surgical bed showed expected post-operative changes with fibrosis and no suspicious soft tissue masses. The liver was without focal lesions. No peritoneal implants. No adenopathy above 1 cm. CA 19-9 was 19 U/mL. CEA 2.0 ng/mL. Assessment: no evidence of disease (NED). Plan for surveillance imaging every 3-4 months for the first 2 years.

At age 67 years (approximately 14 months post-Whipple), the patient was seen for routine surveillance. She reported improved energy and had regained her pre-diagnosis weight. Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in the fingertips persisted but was stable and not worsening. Pancreatic enzyme replacement was optimized. Blood glucose control had improved with endocrinology adjustments to insulin regimen. CA 19-9 was 24 U/mL. CEA 2.2 ng/mL. Performance status ECOG 1. The patient resumed volunteer work at the local library and reported good quality of life. She was counseled on the importance of ongoing surveillance and surveillance CT was scheduled in 3 months.

At age 67 years (approximately 17 months post-Whipple), routine surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast showed a new 1.4 cm hypodense lesion in liver segment VI, with imaging characteristics that could represent a metastatic deposit (rim-enhancing, round, without features of a cyst or hemangioma). No other new lesions. CA 19-9 had risen to 89 U/mL (from a nadir of 18 U/mL). CEA 4.8 ng/mL. Findings were concerning for hepatic metastasis and prompted urgent MRI liver for further characterization.

At age 67 years, MRI liver with hepatobiliary contrast agent (gadoxetate disodium) was performed for characterization of the hepatic lesion. The 1.4 cm segment VI lesion demonstrated hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase, restricted diffusion, and arterial rim enhancement with washout, features diagnostic of metastatic disease to the liver. No additional hepatic lesions were seen. No extrahepatic disease on concurrent CT chest. Findings confirmed solitary liver metastasis consistent with recurrent pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. CA 19-9 at this time: 89 U/mL.

At age 67 years, the patient underwent CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the segment VI hepatic lesion for histologic confirmation. Pathology: metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with pancreatic primary origin. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK19 positive, MUC1 positive, CA 19-9 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, TTF-1 negative, confirming pancreatobiliary origin. Morphology was consistent with the known primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. NGS on the metastatic biopsy confirmed KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R175H, and SMAD4 loss, concordant with the primary tumor profile. No new actionable alterations acquired. Microsatellite stable confirmed. BRCA1/2 somatic mutation: absent.

At age 67 years, the patient was presented at the multidisciplinary tumor board following confirmation of solitary hepatic metastasis from pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, 17 months after curative-intent Whipple procedure. Disease-free interval was 17 months. Performance status ECOG 1. CA 19-9 89 U/mL. Liver function tests were normal. The board discussed oligometastatic disease management. Surgical resection of the liver metastasis was considered but deemed high-risk given prior Whipple, and complete surgical clearance with negative margins was felt uncertain. SBRT to the hepatic lesion was discussed. The consensus recommendation was to initiate systemic chemotherapy for metastatic pancreatic cancer (gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, as the patient had previously received FOLFIRINOX and GemCap in the curative setting), with restaging after 2-3 cycles to assess response and oligometastatic status before considering locoregional therapy. Patient was counseled regarding goals of care transition to disease control, and she agreed to proceed.

At age 67 years, the patient initiated first-line systemic therapy for metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma with gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 and nab-paclitaxel 125 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle. Treatment intent: palliative (disease control, metastatic setting). Gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel was selected given prior exposure to FOLFIRINOX-based and fluoropyrimidine-based regimens in the neoadjuvant and adjuvant settings. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) prophylaxis was not started initially but held as rescue therapy.

scenario_blurb: Locally advanced pancreatic adenocarcinoma (borderline resectable) in a 66-year-old with tumor abutting the SMA and SMV. CA 19-9 elevated to 1200. Treated with neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles with CA 19-9 decline to 85 and partial response on imaging. Then chemoradiation with capecitabine. Successfully undergoes Whipple procedure with negative margins. Adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine complicated by pancreatic fistula.
